reasoning: All three detected peaks lie in the ultra-low frequency region (<50 cm⁻¹), which is characteristic of heavy-atom layered chalcogenides. The dominant peak at 6.74 cm⁻¹ with enormous intensity likely corresponds to a rigid-layer shear or breathing mode of a layered material with very heavy atoms. Bi2Se3 in its bulk rhombohedral phase exhibits low-frequency Raman-active modes: the E_g mode near 37 cm⁻¹ and A1g modes near 72 and 131 cm⁻¹ in standard measurements, but interlayer shear and breathing modes in the ultra-low-frequency regime (below ~40 cm⁻¹) are well documented. The peak at ~43.67 cm⁻¹ is consistent with shifting of the lowest E_g phonon mode, while the peaks at 6.74 and 14.07 cm⁻¹ could be interlayer rigid-layer modes. The narrow linewidths (FWHM ~0.5–2 cm⁻¹) and high SNR indicate a high-quality crystalline sample. However, the absence of the characteristic higher-frequency A1g peaks (~131 and 174 cm⁻¹) in the measured window (only 72 cm⁻¹ range) limits certainty. Alternative heavy-atom layered materials (Bi2Te3, MoS2 in ultra-low-frequency regime) are also possible but the specific peak positions best match Bi2Se3 interlayer modes.
